text:p.text:span: 7.inim amurrum lú kúr-ra åà ma-da-zu ku
8.72,000 åe gur åe dù-a-bi åà ì-si-in
Followed by two fragmentary lines in X1.
ní nu-te ní nu-gíd-i åà-ba Ñá-e nu-te-BU

office:text.text:p: 37.me åúm-mu ús Ñiå-hur ge-en-ge-né kur sig igi-nim-ma
45.[…] x x x x x x hé-en-Ñar igi-bi hé-éb-gi
49.x nu x x x x åà-zu na-an-gig-ge

text:p.text:span: You gave me orders concerning an expedition from Isin to­ Kazallu to purchase grain.
As the market price of grain was equivalent to (one shekel) per liter,
twenty talents of silver was invested in the purchase of grain.
Word having reached me­ that hostile Amorites had entered your frontier territory,
I proceeded to deliver all the grain—72,000 kor—into (the city of) Isin.
But now all ­the Amorites have entered the homeland,
and have captured all the­ great storehouses, one by one.
Because of (these) Amorites I­ cannot hand over the grain for threshing;
Followed by two fragmentary lines in X1.
they are too strong­ for me, and I am made to stay put.
Therefore, my king should (order) the caulking of ­600 barges of 120 liter capacity,
and five (additional) hatches, and have ­all the boats …
that they can be brought down by water, through the Idkura and the Paliåtum canals so they can pile up (the grain) in stacks (for drying),
and I will myself go out to meet him (i.e., Ibbi-Sin).
I shall take responsibility for the place where the boat(s) moor,
so that the 72,000 kor of grain—that is all of the grain—will be placed (there), and be safe.
Should there be a shortage of grain, I will be the one who brings you grain.
Var.: Should you lack grain, I will bring you that grain.
My king is troubled by the war with the Elamite,
but his own grain rations are rapidly being depleted,
so do not release your grip on power,
do­ not rush to become his servant,
There is (enough) grain in my city to provision your palace and all the people for fifteen years,
Var.: I have at my disposal (enough) grain (to provision) your palace and all of its/your cities for fifteen years.
so let the responsibility of guarding the cities of Isin and­ Nippur be mine!
Var.: Please let me guard Isin and Nippur for you, let me have (that) responsibility (o my king)!
Now my king is informed (about all of this)!
My king, I am neither afraid, nor worried, and (if I were you) I would certainly not be
Seek out the intentions of the various gods and attend to them
so that its far-flung people and its supreme populace will be safe and truly
Ur, city of wisdom, linking the heavens and the earth;
having been built by Great Prince (Enki), the exorcist, (it is a city) whose façade is precious (to the people),
one endowed with cosmic rites, whose foundations and ground plans are secure (among all the) lands, south to north,
it will surely be spared and its (favorable divine) decision announced!
(Temple) EkiånuÑal, shrine that envelops the heavens and the earth, without rival—
will not defile it, nor render asunder its guardian deities!
My king, all the loudest noisemakers have run away.
Ibbi-Sin, beloved by the gods as he came out of the womb—
An, Enlil, and Enki, looked so favorably upon him!
Established … so that its front be secure,
are ordering that the doors of the gate of Ur be opened,
But it is you who are the king to whom Enlil gave no rival!
So do not …, so be of good spirits!
, and secured its foundations for you.
Not being … may your spirit be happy!
As long as my king is alive, he will exercise kingship over Ur,
and (I will do) whatever my king might command me to do!
Please, I will not neglect (your orders)!
By Utu, I will not change my allegiance!

All well-preserved sources from outside of Nippur have the longer version. The long version omits the salutation in line 31. Source X5, which has only six lines preserved, could have had either version but has space for the longer one. However, it omits two lines within the preserved section, and therefore it is possible that it originally contained a shorter version of Version B. X4 has the first 29 lines, followed by a double line, that is the short version but without the final salutation. One suspects that it was the first of two tablets with the long version.

The manuscript history of this letter is complex and difficult to trace at present. There seem to be at least two different traditions of the long version, with X1 often at odds with X2 and X3, with some lines quite different and others omitted altogether. The reconstruction presented here is an eclectic text and it could be constructed differently due to the wide range of variants; wherever possible in section A it follows the Nippur manuscripts. There is a long section between lines 15 and 34 where no Nippur text is currently available. Ideally, all eight manuscripts of this composition should be transliterated and translated separately but this is hardly a practical solution here.
Two Nippur manuscripts as well as one of unknown origin have the non-finite form
but a Nippur source (N1), as well as an unprovenanced one (X4), have the finite, albeit somewhat ungrammatical predicate

; in these texts lines 5–6 should read, in translation: “The market price of grain was equivalent to (one shekel) per liter; twenty talents of silver was invested in the purchase of grain.” The expression used here is unique, and is probably a calque from Akkadian. The word
occurs only once in the Ur III documentation (
is used in the same sense as Akkadian
, so that the phrase can either be rendered as “equivalent to,” or “having reached” (
requires comment. It is present in two of the Nippur sources (N2 and N3), while N1 and probably X1 have the more obvious
. It is probable that the form is a calque from the use of
, “to buy,” although that is attested only in Old Assyrian and at Alalakh (
Å /3 30). However, when it is equivalent to
, and one would expect the writing
, “to collect,” for which see C. Wilcke,

18 (1999) 321–22 n. 10 but most examples are reduplicated, and likewise require a for ending in /g/. In that case, perhaps the line means “twenty talents of silver was invested in collecting the purchased of grain.”

. E. Robson, “More than Metrology…” 351, already observed that this letter reads suspiciously like an OB math exercise applied in a semi-realistic context. In view of this, it is particularly troublesome that some of the scribes had problems with the numbers in this text and in the answer IbIå1 (letter 22). The number and the capacity of the barges in line 13, and the recapitulation of the full amount of grain in line 20 indicate that 72,000 liters are at stake but different parts of the letter are reconstructed on the basis of different
manuscripts, and this complicates matters somewhat. The scribe of N3 wrote
, that is, he wrote one inscribed “10” too many. N2 is partially broken at this point; one can see
but there may have been another “10” inscribed here as well. The students are using the “non-mathematical” notation with inscribed
signs, rather that the “mathematical” writing of multiples of
, which is what we find in X4 in line 20.

I am at a loss to explain the mathematical difficulties of the scribes who copied the Iåbi-Erra letters; they should have mastered metrological lists at an earlier stage of their education. For such lists see, for example, E. Robson,
5 (2004) 30–37. But, as she observes on p. 35, students had problems with such lists as well.
. The only full surviving form of the verb among the Nippur texts is ­
(N3). This is clearly incorrect, with the first person ending most likely dittography from the line above.
to designate threshing of grain. M. Civil,
95, has discussed the various terms used in Ur III for this activity; this expression occurs only once in texts from this period (
The syntax and meaning of this line are uncertain. Only one Nippur source has the verb
(N3), which is broken in the other witnesses from that city. The X sources have a different verb—
—but also do not have the first part of the line preserved, and may have had a different text. N1 has
, which may have to be translated differently. This line is crucial for the understanding of the text; see pp.
I know of no similar usages of the two Sumerian verbs in this line. The translation cautiously offered here is based on the idiomatic use of
in Akkadian “to be too strong/much,” although this attested only in later periods (see
D 84 for examples). Note the rare sequence of prefixes
, and translated “I shall ­winnow(?) it.” The reading

. The former usually refers to a ceremonial barge or boat, and it is not clear if the Nippur usage reflects OB literary usage, as such watercraft are well attested in Sumerian poetry, or if there is some irony involved. The non-Nippur texts are more consonant with Ur III texts, which include boats (
capacity. The outfitting of such a boat is detailed in

. If this text is any guide, it took more than 324 talents of bitumen to make such a boat. This means that it would have required more than 194,400 talents to caulk six hundred such vessels—that is 5,832,000 kg. of bitumen! OB references for expenditures of bitumen for boats are collected in D. T. Potts,
, 131–32. I should note that M. Civil,
4 129 has proposed that the word má-gur
, can be a ceremonial barge but also refers to large rafts made of reed bundles, sometimes supported by inflated skins.
is a technical term that refers to the caulking of boats with bitumen but it can also be used in a more general sense for the repair and maintenance of watercraft. This is the case in, for example,
7 31 (BM 18390) where various wood parts are listed for the
of a boat, together with bitumen.
. The state of preservation of these lines, as well as redactional differences, makes it difficult to provide a credible interpretation. Assuming that the 600 boats/barges/rafts of
capacity of line 13 are all that is required to transport 72,000
of grain, the boats mentioned in these lines are military escort vessels.
does not appear in any Sumerian narrative known to me. In Hh 5 104 and 105 (
, that is with the “yoke/crossbeam,” and the “plough,” so perhaps the former is what is meant here. The interpretation cautiously suggested here relates it to
15 20; see also p. 62, l.7’), that is
in X4 is otherwise attested in Sumerian contexts only in OB HAR-ra I 549 and in HAR-ra VI–VII OB Forerunner A 124 (
6 152), known in a variety of different writings (
, with the translation “a rivet or nail.” In light of the following
it is tempting to think of
as a weapon as well, an interpretation that is supported by the listing in OB HAR-ra within a section on martial implements; note also the two in EA 22 i 42 (Tuåratta, cited
164 renders it as “lance,” and derives it, more properly, from Akkadian
3 182, followed by Th. J. H. Krispijn,
70 (1990) 8, proposed that it was a musical instrument, with the name a compound of two instrument names: *
In Ur III the word is written as
; these are clearly martial implements made of wood and copper or bronze, sometimes encrusted with silver.
22 199 11) in a an account of weapons (Ñ
In one case the wood is specified as
3 547:1). The bronze or copper parts were “blades,”
, that is possibly a knob or sharp end (
3 575: 2–3). The more precious ones are given as gifts; e.g.,
, “One bronze z., its wooden part plated in silver, for Ahbabu, the Amorite.” This suggests that
. was a sword rather than a lance.
has been a matter of some debate; for the translation “bow” rather than “throw stick,” see
T 416, although the matter is far from settled.
. It is impossible to identify these watercourses. The­

occurs in some literary texts but it is not always­ certain if this is the name of a canal or its description. The same term is documented in the nineteenth year name of Gungunum of Larsa, and here the meaning is likewise ambiguous, although one suspects that it in indeed a proper name:
tukul ba-ab-sìg ù é-dana bí-in-gi-na ù íd-kur-ra ka-bi ba-an-dib

, “Year: By command of En, Enlil, and Nanna, Malgium was defeated in war and Edana was established, and the opening of the Idkura was crossed.” If this year name can be interpreted in a manner that associates the watercourse with the vicinity of Edana, it may provide a rough geographical location, in Old Babylonian times at least, for Idkura, as the town was probably located east of Isin, on the Iturungal, just upstream of Adab (D. Frayne,
The­ Paliåtum canal is known only from an Emar version of Hh XXII, Msk­74198b iv 12’–13’:

VI.2 484 = VI.4 ­152). None of the OB forerunners, or the later standard recension, have this sequence, and it is possible that these entries are in ­fact quotes from this letter. It is possible that the translations should read: “so that they can be brought down by water, by a mountain river, through the narrows, so they can pile up (the grain) in stacks (for drying).”
is uncertain; the sign is preserved in X4, unavailable for study, and known only from J. van Dijk’s hand copy, in which the inside of the grapheme is shaded. In
. If the reading is correct, then we
, the literary means of describing the spreading/piling up of stacks of grain to dry them for threshing (M. Civil,
is undoubtedly the equivalent of Ur III
that is regularly used for movement of goods up and down watercourses, especially of grain to and from threshing floors; see M. Civil,
92–93, and also the comparable use of Akkadian
. These lines are only preserved in X1 and X4, and are omitted in X5.
. There is some question as to the proper understanding of the syntax of the first half of the line. An alternative interpretation would be: “My king, the Elamite (ruler) has become tired of war…”
is unique. The first is probably “power,” but the second one must be interpreted as
, “hold.” The only possible parallels for this line that I know are in

, “Infants lying in their mother’s laps were swept off like fish (borne by) waters, their strongly protective nursemaids lost their grip (on children),” (also in later bilingual parallels, for which see
, as well as in two OB incantations:
, “The nursemaid’s strong grip (on her charge), released with great force,” and
, “It draws near to the nursemaid with a child in her care and releases her grip (on her charge).”

. The Nippur manuscript N3, which is broken from line 15 on, picks up here, and may have had a somewhat different from the long version. The traces at the beginning of both lines differ, as do the predicates. Perhaps the lines read:
), “don’t even think of putting yourself in his service, certainly, you could not become one of his followers!”
follows a suggestion by Steve Tinney.
. The Elamites are described here as
, which may be interpreted in two interrelated ways. The translation offered here is based lexical entry in Nigga Bil. B 84 (
13 117) where it is rendered in Akkadian as
. However, some OB scribes would undoubtedly been aware of the omens in which Ibbi-Sin is associated with just one word,
in some lexical and bilingual texts (e.g.
in X2 and X3 is possibly to be understood as
; the scribe of X1, who often reinterprets difficult passages, wrote
. This leads to the question of whether the final
is to be interpreted as a variant writing of
or if we are dealing with different traditions here.
is a fascinating allusion to the passage towards the end of the Sargonic section of the
(with variants), “who was king, who was not king?” The Ur
III and some of the OB Nippur versions have this passage in Akkadian but sources from other places, like the texts of the letter, offer the Sumerian language version (P. Steinkeller,

. The line has been lifted from IbPu1: 36 (24), without taking care to change the second person plural suffix into the first person singular, as the new context requires. This error is found in both X2 and X3; the savvy scribe of X1 does not include this line, or the one before it. The verbal root
, “to be negligent.” The expression used here and at the conclusion of the following letter is a calque from the Akkadian
, “please, do not be negligent,” often encountered at the end of Old Babylonian letters (
X1 has two additional fragmentary lines after 11.
The tablet has deteriorated somewhat since it was copied by O. Gurney and since I first collated it.
“Most of the surface is (now) ­in a worse condition than is implied by the copy and I can’t­ really make any specific improvements.”

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets (Type I): X1, X5; all others type III.

text:p.text:span: N2 ++ . ++ + . o o o o o .
X4 xx o o. . . o o + + + +

text:section.text:p: N2 ++ + + + . o o o o o

text:p.text:span: X4 oo o o o x o o . + +

text:section.text:p: X1 o o o o o . o o o o
X4 o o o o o o o o . +

text:note-body.text:p: In all Nippur sources sa10 is consistently written as NÍNDA×ÅE. X1 uses NÍNDA×ÅE.ÀM.

text:section.text:p: X1 o o o o o o o o o an .
X4 o o o o o o o o o . +

text:p.text:span: 5. šakanka aš gur-ta-àm še sá ba-an-dug
X1 o o o o o o . - - di
X4 o o o o o o o . + +
X1 o o o o o . - + . . +
X4 o o o o o o o .
7. inim amurrum lú kúr-ra šà ma-da-zu ku
X1 o o o o o o o . + ku
X4 o o o o o o o . .
8. 72,000 še gur še dù-a-bi šà ì-si-in
N1 o o o o + . . + + + +o o o o . + + .
X4 o o o o o o o o o o o. + . o o o o x

text:note-body.text:p: There may be more missing at the end of the line. If not, then the scribe ended half the line two-thirds into the line and then either started a new or indented line.

text:p.text:span: X4 . o o o o o o o o o o o o o . .­ + +

text:section.text:p: N1 o o o o . . + . o o
X1 o o o o o o x . + +

text:p.text:span: X4 . . o o o o o o o o
o o o o o o
X1 o o o o o o
. o o o o o nu-mu]-un-da-ak-e

text:note-body.text:p: It is not clear if this is a separate line or the indented continuation of the previous one.

text:section.text:p: N1 o o o . + + . o o

text:p.text:span: o o o o o o o
N3 o o o o o. + . - + +
X1 o o o o o b]í-ib-è-
X1 o o o o . . + +ma ši.
N3 o o o o o . . o o o o o :
N3 o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Lines 24 and 25 probably on one line.

text:p.text:span: 28. še mu udilia-ta-àm šà-gal é-gal-zu ù un dù-a-bi šà uru
X1 o o . + . + + + + + + uru + + zu šu-
X1 o o o o o . + + . + . +
X2 x + + + . o o o o . + +
X2 o . . . . o o o o o o . . . .
X2 o x + . o o o + + +
ál èš an-ki šú-šú sukud-rá nu-tuk-a
o o o o o o o o o o x + + .

text:note-body.text:p: The space at the beginning of the line, including a fragment of a sign, suggest that there may have been more here than in X3.

text:p.text:span: X2 o o o o o o o . + +
X1 o o x o o . . ++ + x
X2 o o o o o o o ++ . + + . :
X2 o o o o o o o o x + x x
X2 oo o oo o o . . . . . . . +
X2 o oo o oo . + . x + . + . . o o o
45. [...] x x x x x x hé-en-
X3 o o o o o o o . + . o o . . .
X3 o o o o o o o o . + + + +

text:section.text:p: 49. x nu x x x x šà-zu na-an-gig-ge
X2 x + x x x x + + + + . .
X3 o o o o . + + + + . . o

text:p.text:span: X1 o o o o o o . . . . o o o + x x
